---
title: "Quechua-Spanish Contact"
author: "Eva Corregidor and Robert Esposito"
institute: "Rutgers University"
date: "Last update: `r Sys.Date()`"
format: rutgers-revealjs
engine: knitr
execute: 
  out.width: "100%"
  fig.asp: 0.5625
  dpi: 300
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false


options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE)


knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  # set up some image stuff
  fig.asp = 0.5625,
  out.width = "100%", 
  fig.retina = 2, 
  dpi = 300,
  
  # set up code chunk stuff
  echo = F,
  message = F, 
  warning = F)


set.seed(20250223)

# load libs 
library("tidyverse")
library("here")
library("knitr")
library("flextable")
library("rworldmap")
library("scales")
library("ds4ling")
library("ggtext")

# apply theme
theme_psllt <- function(...) {
  list(
    theme_bw(base_family = "Palatino", ...), 
    theme(
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40"), 
      panel.grid.major = element_line(color = 'grey90', linewidth = 0.15),
      panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = 'grey90', linewidth = 0.15)
    )
  )
}

theme_set(theme_grey(base_size = 30))

```

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false
### Incan Empire map ###
incan_empire <- data.frame(
  country = c("CUB","PRI","CRI","ECU","HND","NIC","PAN",
              "ARG","BOL","CHL","COL","DOM","SLV","GTM",
              "MEX","PRY","PER","URY","VEN"),
  inca = c("no","no","no","yes","no","no","no",
              "yes","yes","yes","yes","no","no","no",
              "no","no","yes","no","no")
)

incan_empire_map_object <- joinCountryData2Map(incan_empire, 
                                              joinCode = "ISO3", 
                                              nameJoinColumn = "country")

# numbers based on census data, Adeelar 1991, Adeelar 2000
# sourced from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quechuan_languages

### Quechua population map ###
quechua_pop <- data.frame(
  country = c("CUB","PRI","CRI",
              "ECU","HND","NIC","PAN",
              "ARG",
              "BOL",
              "CHL",
              "COL","DOM","SLV","GTM","MEX","PRY",
              "PER","URY","VEN"),
  quechua = c("no","no","no","yes","no","no","no",
              "yes","yes","yes","yes","no","no","no",
              "no","no","yes","no","no"),
  num_speakers = c(NA,NA,NA,
                   2300000,NA,NA,NA,
                   900000,
                   2100000,
                   9000,
                   10000,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,
                   3800000,NA,NA)
)

#create map object
quechua_pop_map_object <- joinCountryData2Map(quechua_pop, 
                                              joinCode = "ISO3", 
                                              nameJoinColumn = "country")
```

# The Basics {.transition}

## What are we talking about today?

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {.fragment .semi-fade-out}
History of Quechua-Spanish Contact [@escobar2011spanish]
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {.fragment .semi-fade-out}
An overview of general contact phenomena [@escobar2011spanish]
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {.fragment .semi-fade-out}
Contrastive focus in two Peruvian varieties [@o2012realization]
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {.fragment .semi-fade-out}
Morphosyntactic contact-induced change in Spanish [@sanchez2004functional]
:::
:::

# History of Quechua-Spanish Contact {.transition}

##

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
mapCountryData(
  incan_empire_map_object,
  nameColumnToPlot = "inca",
  mapTitle = "Extension of Incan Empire",
  xlim = c(-80, -55),   # longitude
  ylim = c(-55, 20),     # latitude
  catMethod = "categorical",
  colourPalette = c("white","lightblue"),
  addLegend = FALSE
)

```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# visualize map
mapCountryData(
  quechua_pop_map_object,
  nameColumnToPlot = "num_speakers",
  mapTitle = "Number of Quechua speakers",
  xlim = c(-80, -55),   # longitude
  ylim = c(-55, 20)     # latitude
)

```

:::

::::
- [Incans]{.emph} conquered much of western South America from the [13th to 16th century]{.emph}
- [Quechua became lingua franca]{.emph}, in contact with many languages, among them [Aymara]{.emph}
- Inca elites spoke [extinct Puquina]{.emph} language

::: notes
Quechua = official langues of Incas.
Incan aristocracy used Puquina, now extinct.
Prolonged & intense contact of Quechua, Puquina, and Aymara from 13th to 16th century.
:::

## Spanish Conquest in Western South America

```{r}

# ---- Quechua-Spanish Contact History ----
lingua_franca_df <- tribble(
  ~start_year, ~end_year, ~label, ~y_bottom, ~y_top,
  1438,        1650,      "Quechua lingua franca", 0.7, 0.75,
  1532,        1800,      "Minor Spanish–Quechua bilingualism", 0.62, 0.67,
  1800,        2009,      "Societal Spanish–Quechua bilingualism", 0.7, 0.75,
  1900, 2009, "Emergence of Andean Spanish", 0.62, 0.67
) %>%
  mutate(
    start_date = as.Date(paste0(start_year, "-01-01")),
    end_date   = as.Date(paste0(end_year, "-12-31"))
  )

# ---- Periods ----
periods_df <- tribble(
  ~period,               ~start_year, ~end_year, 
  "Incan Empire",        1438,        1533,
  "Spanish Conquest",    1532,        1572,
  "Colonial Period",       1572,        1820,
  "Modern Era",          1820,        2000
) %>%
  mutate(
    start_date = as.Date(paste0(start_year, "-01-01")),
    end_date   = as.Date(paste0(end_year, "-12-31"))
  )

# ---- Events (spots) ----
spots_df <- tribble(
  ~event,                  ~year,  ~description,
  "Spanish arrival",  1532,  "Spanish arrive in Incan Empire",
  "Urban walls",            1600,  "Walls around main Spanish cities",
  "Language policy change", 1650,  "Spanish king mandates hispanization",
  "Spanish control ends",   1820,  "Political/social control ends"
) %>%
  mutate(
    spot_date = as.Date(paste0(year, "-01-01")),
    # stagger y positions for segments
    label_y = c(1.25, 1.25, 1.25, 1.25),  # vertical position for label
    point_y = 1  # dot inside the bar
  )

# ---- Plot ----
ggplot() +
  # Lang history bar below main bars
  geom_rect(data = lingua_franca_df,
            aes(xmin = start_date, xmax = end_date,
                ymin = y_bottom, ymax = y_top),
            fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_text(data = lingua_franca_df,
            aes(x = start_date + (end_date - start_date)/2,
                y = (y_bottom + y_top)/2, label = label),
            size = 3.5, fontface = "italic") +
  # Main period bars
  geom_rect(data = periods_df,
            aes(xmin = start_date, xmax = end_date,
                ymin = 0.8, ymax = 1.2,
                fill = period),
            color = "black", alpha = 0.5) +
  # Labels inside main bars
  geom_text(data = periods_df,
            aes(x = start_date + (end_date - start_date)/2,
                y = 1, label = period),
            color = "black", size = 4, fontface = "bold",
            angle = 45) +
  # Event points inside the bars
  geom_point(data = spots_df %>% filter(event != "Quechua lingua franca"),
             aes(x = spot_date, y = point_y),
             color = "red", size = 3) +
  # Segments connecting points to labels
  geom_segment(data = spots_df %>% filter(event != "Quechua lingua franca"),
               aes(x = spot_date, xend = spot_date,
                   y = point_y, yend = label_y),
               linetype = "dashed", color = "gray30") +
  # Event labels above the line
  geom_text(data = spots_df %>% filter(event != "Quechua lingua franca"),
            aes(x = spot_date, y = label_y, label = description),
            angle = 25, hjust = 0, size = 4) +
  # X-axis breaks
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "50 years", date_labels = "%Y") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0.65, 1.6)) +
  labs(title = NULL,
       x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal() +
  ds4ling::ds4ling_bw_theme(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

```

::: notes
During colonial period, Quechua used as lingua franca.
Of the Spanish, merchants, administrators, and clergymen learned Quechua.
Of the Andeans, children of indigenous elite, clergymen, & escribanos (helpers) learned Spanish.

Walls constructed in early 17th century to separate Spaniards & indigenous -- very restricted contact.
:::

## Modern Language Policies

```{r}
# ---- Modern language policy events ----
policy_events <- tribble(
  ~country,   ~year, ~policy,
  "Colombia", 1991,  "Spanish official; indigenous langs official regionally",
  "Peru",     1993,  "Right to use Spanish or indigenous languages in official gov't proceedings",
  "Chile",    1993,  "Chilean Indigenous Law: recognition of Amerindian languages",
  "Ecuador",  2008,  "Preservation of indigenous languages",
  "Bolivia",  2009,  "Spanish & all indigenous languages official",
  "Argentina", 2000, "No reference to indigenous languages"
) %>%
  mutate(event_date = as.Date(paste0(year, "-01-01")))

# ---- Plot ----
ggplot(policy_events, aes(x = event_date, y = country)) +
  geom_point(data = subset(policy_events, country != "Argentina"),
             color = "red", size = 4) +  
  geom_text(aes(label = str_wrap(policy, width = 40)),
            hjust = -0.05, vjust = 0.5, size = 4) +
  scale_x_date(
    date_breaks = "5 years",
    date_labels = "%Y",
    limits = as.Date(c("1990-01-01", "2015-12-31"))
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev(c("Colombia","Peru","Chile","Ecuador","Bolivia","Argentina"))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  ds4ling::ds4ling_bw_theme(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

::: notes
Indigenous languages are being recognized across LATAM, but of course there's still a lot of stigma.
Despite the gov't language policies to support them, there is still a growing shift from Quechua to Spanish, especially as Quechua speakers move to urban centers.
:::

## Andean Spanish

::: {.fragment}
- Andean Spanish is [20th century phenomenon]{.emph}
- Only two other cases of bilingualism in the Americas: Spanish-English in the US, and French-English in Canada
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Features that distinguish this dialect from other varieties of Spanish (interlect (A. Escobar 1976, 1978), castellano motoso (Cerrón 1990, 2003))
[interlect; @escobar1976bilingualism; @escobar1978variaciones], castellano motoso [@@cerron1990aspectos; @cerron2003castellano]
:::

## Andean Spanish

::: {.fragment}
- a [Spanish variety]{.emph} primarily spoken in the [Andean region]{.emph} of various countries spoken by...
:::

::: {.fragment}
    - monolingual speakers
    
:::

::: {.fragment}
    - 2L1 bilinguals 

:::

::: {.fragment}
    - L1 Quechua or L1 Aymara L2 Spanish speakers

:::

:::{.fragment}
- daily contact with [L2 Andean Spanish]{.emph} speakers
:::

:::{.fragment}
- contact with [non-Andean varieties]{.emph} of Spanish
:::

## Andean Spanish Outside of the Andes

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

![](assets/quechua_in_usa.bmp){ width=80% }

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

- Large Quechua-speaking population in Queens & Brooklyn in NYC
- Large Quechua-speaking population in Patterson, NJ
  - Largest Peruvian percentage in the U.S. per capita!
  
:::
::::

::: footer
https://languagemap.nyc/

https://web.archive.org/web/20150201040607/http://www.utsandiego.com/news/2011/may/28/some-ny-immigrants-cite-lack-of-spanish-as-barrier/

@paerregaard2008peruvians
:::

::: notes
Quechua varieties are spoken in Brooklyn, Queens, and for some reason concentrated in Patterson, NJ.
:::

## Let's listen to two varieties

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

{{< youtube K2iYeGTwcU8 >}}

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

{{< youtube b_DtKXDXLFY >}}

:::
::::

- Anything that catches your attention?

## {background-iframe="https://ich.unesco.org/doc/src/00120-EN.pdf" background-interactive=TRUE}

# General Contact Phonema {.transition}

## Let's review some terminology...

- Van Coestem [-@coetsem2000general; -@van2016loan] proposes a psycholinguistic model for borrowing...

::: {.fragment}
In groups, define the following terms and be prepared to share a [specific contact example]{.emph}
:::

::: {.fragment}
- Source language
- Recipient language
- Agentivity
- Imposition
- Borrowing
:::

::: notes
All the borrowings and other changes that we'll see have either been identified in at least 2 out of the four main contact areas (Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador and northern Argentina), and/or have not been widely studied
:::

## The Lexicon

::: {.fragment}
- Lexical borrowings are common in contact situations, especially [cultural borrowings]{.emph}
:::

::: {.fragment}
- Most borrowings entered Spanish during the colonial period, mainly [nouns]{.emph}
:::

::: {.fragment}
- We know of them through [chronicles]{.emph} and other documents of the time 
:::

## The Lexicon

```{r}
data.frame(
  matrix(
    c(
      "huayco - mud slide",
      "palta - avocado",
      "ñano - male child",
      "cuy - guinea pig",
      "pisco - grape brandy",
      "zapallo - squash",
      
      "condor - condor",
      "caracha - skin infection",
      "quinua - quinoa",
      "tambo - small inn",
      "ojota - sandal",
      "choclo - corn",
      
      "pampa - plateau",
      "guagua - infant",
      "charqui - dried meat",
      "puna - high altitudes",
      "alpaca - alpaca",
      "carpa - tent",
      
      "canchita - popcorn",
      "soroche - altitude sickness",
      "pucho - cigarette butt",
      "papa - potato",
      "caucho - rubber",
      "concho - sediment",
      
      "quena - quena",
      "chancar - to flatten",
      "cancha - large outdoor space",
      "calato - naked",
      "puma - puma",
      "chakra - ranch"
    ),
    nrow = 5,
    ncol = 6,
    byrow = TRUE
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
) %>%
  kable(col.names = NULL)

```

::: note
@escobar2011spanish
:::

::: notes
Quechua cultural borrowings commonly refer to the flora, fauna, local social organization, food, agriculture, clothing, folklore, and religion
:::

## Grammatical Borrowing

- Quechua plural marker for nouns, -kuna (2)
- First person possessive marker (3)
- The diminutive -cha (4)

![](assets/gram_borr_1.png)

## Grammatical Borrowing

- The derivative marker used for courtesy -ri (6)
- The negative emphatic -tan (7)

![](assets/gram_borr_2.png)

## The Segmental Inventory of Cusco Quechua

```{r}
consonant_inventory <- data.frame(
  Category = c(
    "Nasal",
    "Stop/Affricate (plain)",
    "Stop/Affricate (aspirated)",
    "Stop/Affricate (ejective)",
    "Fricative",
    "Semivowel",
    "Liquid (lateral)",
    "Liquid (rhotic)"
  ),
  Bilabial = c(
    "m",
    "p",
    "pʰ",
    "pʼ",
    "",
    "",
    "",
    ""
  ),
  Alveolar = c(
    "n",
    "t",
    "tʰ",
    "tʼ",
    "s",
    "",
    "l",
    "ɾ"
  ),
  `Post-alv./Palatal` = c(
    "ɲ",
    "tʃ",
    "tʃʰ",
    "tʃʼ",
    "ʃ",
    "j",
    "ʎ",
    ""
  ),
  Velar = c(
    "",
    "k",
    "kʰ",
    "kʼ",
    "",
    "w",
    "",
    ""
  ),
  Uvular = c(
    "",
    "q",
    "qʰ",
    "qʼ",
    "",
    "",
    "",
    ""
  ),
  Glottal = c(
    "",
    "",
    "",
    "",
    "h",
    "",
    "",
    ""
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

knitr::kable(consonant_inventory, align = "lcccccc")
```

::: note
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cusco_Quechua
:::

## Let's take a listen... {.scrollable}

![](assets/andean_spanish.mp4)

## Morphosyntactic Features

- Use of conditional in protasis/apodosis of conditional sentences (8)
- Omission of third person object clitics (9)
- Use of animate leísmo (10)

![](assets/morphosynt_1.png)

## Morphosyntactic Features

- Frequent use of diminutive on nouns, adj, gerunds, numerals, adverbs, pronouns (modesty)([corriendito, lejitos, ellita]{.emph})
- Frequent use of titles with first names ([Doctor Gustavo, Señor Pedro, Licenciado Juan]{.emph})
- Redundant direct object clitics with nominal referent ([lo visité a mi papá]{.emph})

## Semantic and Discursive Features

- Use of estar + GERUND with the present function ([¿en qué parte de Estados Unidos está viviendo Susana?]{.emph})

## Typology

- Quechua is [agglutinative]{.emph}; Spanish is [fusional]{.emph}

- Quechua likes to add [suffixes]{.emph} to express meaning, sort of like Spanish, but there's usually a one-to-one correspondence of morpheme-to-meaning

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
[Spanish]{.emph}  

<span style="color:#8c564b;">aún</span>
<span style="color:#1f77b4;">estoy</span>
<span style="color:#d62728;">com</span>
<span style="color:#1f77b4;">iendo</span>

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
[Quechua]{.emph}  

<span style="color:#d62728;">mikhu</span>
<span style="color:#1f77b4;">chka</span>
<span style="color:#ffbf00;">ni</span>
<span style="color:#8c564b;">raq</span>
<span style="color:#17becf;">mi</span>
:::

::::

## Typology

- Quechua is <span style="color:#8c564b;">s</span><span style="color:#1f77b4;">o</span><span style="color:#d62728;">v</span>; Spanish is <span style="color:#8c564b;">s</span><span style="color:#d62728;">o</span><span style="color:#1f77b4;">v</span>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
[Spanish]{.emph}  

<span style="color:#8c564b;">nosotros</span> <span style="color:#d62728;">comemos</span> <span style="color:#1f77b4;">carne</span>

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
[Quechua]{.emph}  

<span style="color:#8c564b;">ñuqanchik</span> <span style="color:#1f77b4;">aychata</span> <span style="color:#d62728;">mikhunchik</span>
:::

::::

## Patterns of Use

<span style="color:#d62728;">Usqhaylla</span> <span style="color:#1f77b4;">phawarqayku</span>

<span style="color:#1f77b4;">We ran</span> <span style="color:#d62728;">quickly</span>

<span style="color:#d62728;">Kimsa horatam</span> <span style="color:#1f77b4;">mikunchik</span>

<span style="color:#1f77b4;">We eat</span> <span style="color:#d62728;">at three o'clock</span>

::: {.fragment}
- What might Andean Spanish speakers produce?
:::

## Discuss...

- According to [generativist/usage-based] theories, changes in patterns of use [is]{.emph} reflective of language change

::: {.fragment}
- What motivates the [generativist]{.emph} belief that patterns of usage [is not]{.emph} reflective of language change?
- What motivates the [usage-based]{.emph} belief that patterns of usage [is]{.emph} reflective of language change?
:::

# Pragmatics in Contact [@o2012realization] {.transition}

## Pasto, Colombia

{{< youtube udRn6DTwEIk >}}

## Discuss...

- Define and give an example of [broad focus]{.emph} and [contrastive focus]{.emph}

::: fragment
- What are some [linguistic strategies]{.emph} to express [contrastive focus]{.emph} in [Spanish]{.emph} and [Quechua]{.emph}?
:::

::: fragment
- Based on the [Maximize Common Ground]{.smallcaps} [@hawkins2024bilingualism] principle, what hypotheses can we make about [expressing contrastive focus]{.emph} in Andean Spanish?
:::

::: fragment
- Who are the [participants]{.emph}?
- [Why]{.emph} were these participants chosen?
:::

## Draw...

![](assets/limonada.png)

1. On a piece of paper, write the above sentence twice.
2. Demonstrate what a change in [peak height]{.emph} would look like on the [verb]{.emph}.
3. Demonstrate what a change in [peak aligment]{.emph} would look like on the [verb]{.emph}.

## Peak Alignment {.scrollable}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

[Lima]{style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

![](assets/lima_alignment.bmp)

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

[Cuzco Quechua-Spanish bilingual]{style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

![](assets/cuzco_bilingual_alignment.bmp)

:::

::::

```{r}
data.frame(
  City = c("Lima",
           "Lima",
           "Lima",
           "Lima",
           "Lima",
           "Lima",
           
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco"),
  
  Speaker = c("L01",
              "L02",
              "L03",
              "L04",
              "L05",
              "L06",
              
              "C01",
              "C08",
              "C09",
              
              "C21",
              "C23",
              
              "C33"),
  
  Language = c("L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               
               "Quechua-Spanish bilingual",
               "Quechua-Spanish bilingual",
               
               "L1 Quechua L2 Spanish"
  ),
  Subject = c("*",
              "*",
              "*",
              "*",
              "nsd",
              "*",
              
              "nsd",
              "*",
              "*",
              
              "nsd",
              "nsd",
              
              "nsd")
) %>% flextable() %>%
  merge_v(j = c("City","Language")) %>%
  valign(j = c("City","Language"), valign = "center") %>%
  hline(i = 6, border = officer::fp_border(width = 1.5)) %>%
  hline(i = 9, 
        border = officer::fp_border(width = 1, style = "dotted")) %>%
  hline(i = 11, 
        border = officer::fp_border(width = 1, style = "dotted"))

```

## Peak Height {.scrollable}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

[Lima]{style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

![](assets/lima_height.bmp)

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

[Cuzco L1 Spanish monolingual]{style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

![](assets/cuzco_l1-spanish_height.bmp)

[Cuzco Quechua-Spanish bilingual]{style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

![](assets/cuzco_bilingual_height.bmp)

[Cuzco L1 Quechua L2 Spanish]{style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

![](assets/cuzco_l1-quechua_height.bmp)

:::

::::

```{r}
data.frame(
  City = c("Lima",
           "Lima",
           "Lima",
           "Lima",
           "Lima",
           "Lima",
           
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco",
           "Cuzco"),
  
  Speaker = c("L01",
              "L02",
              "L03",
              "L04",
              "L05",
              "L06",
              
              "C01",
              "C05",
              "C06",
              "C07",
              "C09",
              
              "C21",
              "C22",
              "C23",
              "C25",
              
              "C31",
              "C32",
              "C33"),
  
  Language = c("L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               "L1 Spanish",
               
               "Quechua-Spanish bilingual",
               "Quechua-Spanish bilingual",
               "Quechua-Spanish bilingual",
               "Quechua-Spanish bilingual",
               
               "L1 Quechua L2 Spanish",
               "L1 Quechua L2 Spanish",
               "L1 Quechua L2 Spanish"
  ),
  Subject = c("*",
              "nsd",
              "nsd",
              "nsd",
              "nsd",
              "*",
              
              "nsd",
              "*",
              "nsd",
              "*",
              "*",
              
              "nsd",
              "*",
              "*",
              "*",
              
              "*",
              "*",
              "*")
  
) %>% flextable() %>%
  merge_v(j = c("City","Language")) %>%
  valign(j = c("City","Language"), valign = "center") %>%
  hline(i = 6, border = officer::fp_border(width = 1.5)) %>%
  hline(i = 11, 
        border = officer::fp_border(width = 1, style = "dotted")) %>%
  hline(i = 15, 
        border = officer::fp_border(width = 1, style = "dotted"))

```

## Discuss...

::: {.fragment}

- Were the author's [hypotheses]{.emph} supported?

- According to the [criteria]{.emph} proposed by @thomason2019language, is there enough [evidence]{.emph} to propose contact-induced language change?

:::

::: {.fragment}

- [What more information]{.emph} would we need to decide if this is contact-induced language change?

:::

# Morphosyntax in Contact [@sanchez2004functional] {.transition}

## Functional Convergence Hypothesis (FCH)

::: {.fragment}

- The FCH proposes that languages tend to converge (that is, become more similar) when they share similar functional features, rather than "only" words or sounds

:::

::: {.fragment}
- How do languages encode meaning through grammar? (tense, agreement, word order, definiteness, etc.)
:::

::: {.fragment}
- Goal: do bilinguals keep grammars fully separate or not?
:::

## Functional Convergence Hypothesis (FCH)

- Possible scenarios:

::: {.fragment}
    - Languages share the structures, and so reinforce each other (positive transfer)
    
:::

::: {.fragment}
    - Languages have different structures, and so their systems are reshaped
    
:::

## The Study

[Research Question]{.emph}: How do bilingual Quechua–Spanish children mix grammatical features across their two languages?

![](assets/sanchez_table1.png)

::: notes
aspect (sp) vs source of knowledge (qu, evidentiality)
:::

## Tense in Quechua
- -rqa = witnessed (direct evidence)

![](assets/rqa.png)

## Tense in Quechua

- -sqa = reported (hearsay)

![](assets/sqa.png)

## Predictions?

If there is convergence, then bilingual speakers will...

::: {.fragment}
    - Rely more on evidentiality when speaking Spanish
    
:::

::: {.fragment}
    - Rely more on aspect when speaking Quechua
    
:::

## Methods. Participants

![](assets/participants.png)

## Methods. Tasks
- Story re-telling task (both oral and written, although only analyzed oral)

## Results

![](assets/group1.png)

## Results

![](assets/group2.png)

## Discussion

SP-QU bilinguals...

- Begin to encode foreground vs background when speaking Quechua (influence from Spanish)
- Use tense differently from monolinguals when speaking Spanish (influence from Quechua)

::: notes
Imperfect & pluperfect used as EVIDENTIAL
Overuse of imperfect forms
Present perfect = foreground + evidence
:::

## Discussion

- Spanish monolinguals: [tense + aspect]{.emph}
- Spanish in bilinguals: [tense + aspect + evidentiality]{.emph}

- Quechua monolinguals: [tense + evidentiality]{.emph}]
- Quechua bilinguals: [tense + evidentiality + aspect/discourse]{.emph}]


## Thanks! {.final visibility="uncounted"}

![](https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/i/teamlogos/ncaa/500/164.png){.absolute top="0" right="0" width="55" height="55"}



<table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: none;">

  <tr>
    <td style="text-align: right; padding-right: 10px;">
      <a href="https://www.jvcasillas.com/quarto-rutgers-theme/">{{< fa solid link >}}</a>
    </td>
    <td>jvcasillas.com/quarto-rutgers-theme</td>
  </tr>
  
  <tr>
    <td style="text-align: right; padding-right: 10px;">
      <a href="mailto:rme70@scarletmail.rutgers.edu">{{< fa solid envelope >}}</a>
    </td>
    <td>rme70@scarletmail.rutgers.edu</td>
  </tr>

  <tr>
    <td style="text-align: right; padding-right: 10px;">
      <a href="https://robertespo.github.io/">{{< fa solid link >}}</a>
    </td>
    <td>robertespo.github.io</td>
  </tr>

  <tr>
    <td style="text-align: right; padding-right: 10px;">
      <a href="https://github.com/RobertEspo">{{< fa brands github >}}</a>
    </td>
    <td>\@RobertEspo</td>
  </tr>
  
  <tr>
    <td style="text-align: right; padding-right: 10px;">
      <a href="mailto:ec1310@scarletmail.rutgers.edu">{{< fa solid envelope >}}</a>
    </td>
    <td>ec1310@scarletmail.rutgers.edu</td>
  </tr>
  
  <tr>
    <td style="text-align: right; padding-right: 10px;">
      <a href="emcorregidor.github.io/">{{< fa solid link >}}</a>
    </td>
    <td>emcorregidor.github.io/</td>
  </tr>
  
  <tr>
    <td style="text-align: right; padding-right: 10px;">
      <a href="https://github.com/emcorregidor">{{< fa brands github >}}</a>
    </td>
    <td>\@emcorregidor</td>
  </tr>
  
</table>

# Extra Slides {visibility="uncounted"}

## Contact-Induced Language Change Criteria [@thomason2019language] {.scrollable}

1. The recipient language must be examined as a whole, not single features in isolation. Multiple related features in Language B must point to external influence.

2. There must be a plausible source Language A that was in sustained and intimate contact with Language B.

3. A and B must share several features across different subsystems (e.g., syntax, morphology, phonology), not just a single feature.

4. The feature must be old in Language A (i.e., not an innovation in the source language).

5. The feature must be innovative (new) in Language B (i.e., not present in B before contact).


## References {visibility="uncounted"}

::: {#refs .smaller}
:::
